text:
Centre for Neurology Services

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 11 January 2021

Dr A. Rahman
Consultant Neurologist
Riverside Neurology Clinic
12 Millbank, London SW1P 3GE

Dear Dr A. Rahman,

Ms Priya Nair, DOB: 22-09-1991, Hospital No: RN783642 NHS No. 4082165973
Flat 5, 9 Willow Court, Birmingham B1 2AB

Diagnosis:

Seizure disorder; current control variable. Levetiracetam previously trialled without benefit. Lamotrigine currently continued.

Aetiology:

Undetermined.

Other Medical Problems:

Bilateral hearing difficulty; patient prefers scheduled speakerphone calls with a family member assisting communication.

Management Plan:

1) Continue Lamotrigine 100 mg mane and 150 mg nocte. Maintain single-brand supply. 2) Arrange serum level check in 6 weeks and review thereafter. 3) Provide written seizure first-aid guidance for family and workplace. 4) Offer travel risk-mitigation advice and fit note to support adjustments if required. 5) Book follow-up via scheduled speakerphone with nominated relative present; in-person review available if communication support arranged.

Clinical Summary:

Ms Priya Nair attended with a family member assisting via speakerphone, in line with their communication preference due to hearing difficulty. They report two Travel-related clusters this month; ~6 events per episode, each event lasting under a minute with brief post-event confusion and no injuries. Triggers were noted around long journeys and airport transfers. There have been no nocturnal events reported. Adherence to Lamotrigine is good; no rash or mood change. Past trial of Levetiracetam was ineffective per patient report. No recent intercurrent illness, alcohol overuse, or sleep deprivation beyond travel days. Neurological examination today was non-focal. ECG last year was normal; routine bloods earlier this year unremarkable.

We discussed strategies to reduce cluster risk on travel days (rest breaks, hydration, avoidance of missed doses, and ensuring companions are aware of first aid). The patient prefers non-urgent communications via scheduled speakerphone with a family member present; clinic will accommodate this.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: two Travel-related clusters this month; ~6 events per episode
two Travel-related clusters this month; ~6 events per episode

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter reports: "two Travel-related clusters this month; ~6 events per episode". This provides an explicit monthly count of clusters and the number of events per cluster. No other frequencies are given that would supersede this. Therefore, the seizure frequency is 2 clusters per month with approximately 6 seizures per cluster.
